questions.question: Explain the term 'Single-Board Computer' (SBC) and why Raspberry Pi is classified as an SBC.
keyPoints: Definition of SBC: Complete computer on single circuit board
Contains CPU, GPU, memory, I/O ports all integrated
No separate components needed like traditional desktops
Raspberry Pi has all components on one compact board

questions.question: List and explain four major connectivity options available on modern Raspberry Pi models.
keyPoints: WiFi: Wireless networking (802.11n/ac)
Bluetooth: Wireless device communication (4.1/5.0)
Ethernet: Wired networking (10/100/1000 Mbps)
USB: Connect peripherals and sensors
HDMI: Display output

questions.question: What is the function of GPIO pins in Raspberry Pi? Why are they important for IoT applications?
keyPoints: GPIO = General Purpose Input/Output pins
Provide digital input/output capabilities
Support communication protocols: I2C, SPI, UART
Enable connection to sensors, actuators, LEDs
Essential for physical world interaction in IoT

questions.question: Differentiate between CSI and DSI ports on Raspberry Pi with their respective uses.
keyPoints: CSI: Camera Serial Interface - for camera module connection
DSI: Display Serial Interface - for touchscreen connection
CSI used for image/video capture applications
DSI used for interactive display projects
Both enable expandability for IoT projects

questions.question: What is a System on Chip (SoC)? Explain its significance in Raspberry Pi architecture.
keyPoints: SoC integrates multiple components on single chip
Contains CPU, GPU, memory controller, I/O peripherals
Reduces board size and power consumption
Raspberry Pi uses Broadcom SoCs (BCM2835, BCM2836, BCM2711)
Enables compact, cost-effective computing platform

questions.question: Describe the power requirements of Raspberry Pi and explain why a stable power supply is critical.
keyPoints: Requires 5V DC power input
Current requirement: 2.5-3A for stable operation
Power via micro-USB (or USB-C on Pi 4)
Insufficient power causes instability, crashes, SD card corruption
Quality power supply essential for IoT reliability

questions.question: Describe the evolution of Raspberry Pi hardware from Model B to Raspberry Pi 4, highlighting the key improvements in each generation that make it suitable for IoT applications.
keyPoints: Model B (2012): ARM11, 256MB RAM, 2 USB ports, no WiFi/BT
Model B+ (2014): 40-pin GPIO, better power management, 4 USB ports
Pi 2 (2015): Quad-core ARM Cortex-A7, 1GB RAM
Pi 3 (2016): Built-in WiFi/Bluetooth, 64-bit processor
Pi 4 (2019): ARM Cortex-A72, up to 8GB RAM, USB-C, Gigabit Ethernet
Each generation improved processing power, connectivity, memory for better IoT performance

questions.question: Design an IoT-based temperature monitoring system using Raspberry Pi. Explain the hardware components required, their connections, and how the system would work.
keyPoints: Hardware: Raspberry Pi, temperature sensor (DS18B20), microSD card, power supply
Connect sensor to GPIO pins (digital output)
Install OS on microSD card
Write Python script to read sensor data
Connect to network for data transmission
Use cloud dashboard or local server for monitoring
GPIO pins enable physical world interaction
WiFi/Ethernet enables data transmission

questions.question: Explain the architecture of Raspberry Pi focusing on the relationship between CPU, GPU, and memory components. How does this architecture support IoT applications?
keyPoints: SoC integrates CPU (processing), GPU (graphics/video), memory controller
CPU handles general computing tasks and sensor data processing
GPU accelerates video encoding/decoding for camera applications
RAM provides working memory for applications
microSD provides persistent storage for OS and data
GPIO bridges CPU to physical sensors/actuators
Network interfaces enable connectivity
This architecture supports: data collection, processing, storage, and transmission - all essential for IoT
